briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon/evening. Welcome to the climate briefing for April 10, 2026. Today, we will examine the severe warnings about the climate crisis, along with various domestic and international efforts and challenges to overcome it.

sections.title: 🔴 Deepening Climate Crisis and Ecosystem Warnings
sections.content: News released today once again reminds us that the impacts of climate change on our ecosystems and society are severe and widespread. The International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) has officially designated emperor penguins and Antarctic fur seals as endangered species (EN), warning of a crisis in the Antarctic ecosystem [2]. Domestically, mass die-offs of coniferous trees due to the climate crisis are occurring, with growing concerns particularly regarding the situation in Korea [4, 5]. Furthermore, climate change has been likened to a 'black swan,' with calls for urgent efforts [3]. Extreme weather events are intensifying globally, with marine heatwaves nearly doubling the economic damage from tropical cyclones and exacerbating floods in the Chicago area [76, 83]. This situation, coupled with repeated warnings from the scientific community that climate change is outpacing evolutionary speed, underscores the urgency of the crisis we face [39, 54, 60].

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Strengthening Domestic Carbon Neutrality Practices and Policies
sections.content: Amidst this crisis, various efforts to transition to a carbon-neutral society continue domestically. Gimhae City operated special carbon neutrality events during Climate Change Week [1]. Gwangju City launched the 'Bitgoeul Net-Zero Friends' citizen action group and is expanding carbon neutrality practices through its second phase of activities [10, 12]. Busan City Council is actively pursuing a circular economy and carbon neutrality through the recycling of discarded shoes [9]. Jeonju City also designated a 'Zero Disposable Products' day to conduct a carbon neutrality practice campaign [14]. The active participation of local governments is notable, with Anseong City and Pohang City also reviewing their carbon neutrality progress [7, 13]. Practical measures to encourage citizen participation, such as a weekend discount policy for public EV charging fees, have also been introduced [25].

sections.title: 🌱 Accelerating Renewable Energy Transition and Current Challenges
sections.content: The government and industry are accelerating the transition to renewable energy. The Ministry of Climate has finalized a supplementary budget of 616.2 billion KRW, expanding support for renewable energy transition and vulnerable populations, with plans to specifically invest 368.3 billion KRW into renewable energy transition [15, 17, 18, 21, 22, 27, 28]. This is a crucial step signaling a major transformation of Korean industry to achieve the 20% renewable energy target by 2030 [24]. However, concerns have also been raised regarding power regulation issues stemming from renewable energy expansion and the perception that 'less bad' is not equivalent to 'eco-friendly' [19, 20]. Various discussions on the direction of energy policy are ongoing, with the Korean Nuclear Society, for instance, urging a shift from a 'renewable energy-centric transition' to a 'carbon-free energy-centric transition' [23]. The availability of renewable energy and electricity prices are also identified as key factors determining success in the decarbonization and electrification process of the petrochemical industry [29, 31], emphasizing the need for integrated strategies beyond mere technological demonstration [11].

sections.title: 🌍 International Community's Climate Change Research and Policy Trends
sections.content: The international community expresses deep concern about the pace of climate change and is seeking scientific research and policy responses. Numerous media outlets, including the Associated Press (AP), have reported that climate change is outpacing the evolutionary speed of organisms, and scientists are striving to adapt ecosystems to climate change using DNA technology [32, 37, 39, 41, 64, 65]. This suggests that climate change has reached a level where it threatens not just environmental issues but also the fundamental adaptive capacity of life itself. Meanwhile, in the United States, discussions are underway regarding the impact of the Trump administration's Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) overturning a landmark climate change ruling on communities, including Illinois [35, 38]. Regional climate impact studies, such as research on Alaska's coastline changes, are also actively being conducted [36]. These international trends provide important implications for enhancing scientific understanding of climate change and exploring effective policy directions.

briefing.closing: Today's briefing has highlighted the grave reality of the climate crisis while also showcasing diverse domestic and international efforts to overcome it. Only sustained attention and action can safeguard the future of humanity and our planet. This concludes today's climate briefing.
